plan.overall_rationale: Dropped ID-like and free-text/high-cardinality columns (PassengerId, Name, Ticket) that carry no generalizable signal, and Cabin due to extreme missingness. Kept all other informative features including numeric measurements (Age, Fare) and categorical/ordinal columns, imputing missing values with median (Age) and mode (Embarked) to preserve as much data as possible without introducing bias.

features.reason: Combining siblings/spouses and parents/children counts can approximate family dynamics; the difference may capture relative composition of family aboard, complementing family size.
Fare relative to class may reveal whether a passenger paid more or less than typical for their class, indicating cabin quality or group discounts.
Fare is a non-negative, right-skewed monetary variable; log1p transform can stabilize variance and help linear-type learners capture its effect.
Age likely has non-linear relationship with survival (e.g., children prioritized); quantile binning can help tree-based models capture threshold effects more robustly.
Fare has a wide range and skew; binning into quantile groups can highlight socio-economic tiers correlated with survival beyond raw magnitude.

feature_plan.overall_rationale: The Titanic dataset's key survival drivers are socio-economic status (Pclass, Fare), family composition (SibSp, Parch), and demographics (Age, Sex). I propose derived features that capture family size dynamics (difference of SibSp and Parch), relative fare within class (ratio), a log transform of the skewed Fare variable, and quantile bins for Age and Fare to expose non-linear thresholds (e.g., children, wealth tiers) that AutoGluon's tree-based models can exploit. No date columns exist, so date_parts is not applicable. Sex and Embarked are categorical and left for AutoGluon's native encoding.

plan.overall_rationale: Only the pure row-index 'Id' column is dropped as it carries no predictive signal. All other columns, including high-missingness categoricals like PoolQC, Fence, MiscFeature, and Alley, are retained since their missingness is semantically meaningful (absence of a feature) and AutoGluon can leverage this signal once imputed with an explicit 'None' category. Numeric columns with missing values (LotFrontage, MasVnrArea, GarageYrBlt) are imputed with median to preserve distribution robustness. Electrical's single missing value is filled with the mode. No leakage-prone or ID-like columns beyond 'Id' were identified.

features.reason: Age of house at time of sale is a strong predictor of price; combines two year columns into an interpretable house-age feature.
Years since last remodel captures how 'fresh' the house is, often correlating with sale price independent of original build year.
Difference between above-ground living area and basement area highlights houses with disproportionate basement vs living space.
Garage size relative to living area normalizes garage footprint by house size, useful for comparing across different house scales.
Ratio of basement to above-ground area captures house layout style, which can affect valuation.
LotArea is a non-negative, highly skewed numeric feature; log1p transform reduces skew and stabilizes variance for tree-based models.
GrLivArea is non-negative and right-skewed; log transform can help linear/tree models capture diminishing marginal effect on price.
1st floor square footage is non-negative and skewed; log1p transform normalizes distribution for better model fit.
Binning the overall quality rating into quantile groups can help capture non-linear threshold effects on price beyond raw ordinal value.
Grouping construction year into quantile bins can capture era-based effects (e.g., building codes, architectural styles) on price.

feature_plan.overall_rationale: The plan focuses on well-known drivers of house price: age since build/remodel, relative sizing ratios between living/basement/garage areas, and log-transforming skewed area-based numeric features to reduce heteroscedasticity. Binning OverallQual and YearBuilt captures potential non-linear era or quality tier effects. All engineered features avoid using the target column and rely only on numeric, non-ID columns from the fixed vocabulary of transformations.

proposal.rationale: SalePrice is a continuous, non-negative regression target with 663 distinct values. It is clearly right-skewed: mean (180,921) noticeably exceeds median (163,000), skewness is 1.88, and the max (755,000) is over 4.6x the median with a long tail of expensive homes. Training on log1p(SalePrice) will make squared-error loss relative rather than dominated by a few high-priced outliers, which typically improves performance on this well-known heteroscedastic price target.

It is clearly right-skewed: mean (180,921) noticeably exceeds median (163,000), skewness is 1.88, and the max (755,000) is over 4.6x the median with a long tail of expensive homes. Training on log1p(SalePrice) will make squared-error loss relative rather than dominated by a few high-priced outliers, which typically improves performance on this well-known heteroscedastic price target.

feature_plan.overall_rationale: The dataset consists of continuous numeric features, which are suitable for transformations like ratios, differences, and log transformations. These transformations can help capture relative and absolute differences, as well as normalize skewed data, potentially enhancing the predictive power of the model.

proposal.rationale: The target 'y' is continuous and non-negative, which are necessary conditions for considering a log1p transformation. However, the distribution is not clearly right-skewed. The mean (14.0877) is very close to the median (14.0685), and the maximum (29.089) is not several times the median, indicating a lack of a long right tail. Additionally, the skewness is very low (0.048), suggesting the distribution is roughly symmetric. Therefore, a log1p transformation is not appropriate.

plan.overall_rationale: The 'rowid' column is an ID-like column with unique values for each row, which does not contribute to the predictive power of the model. The 'text' column, despite its high cardinality, is likely to contain valuable information for predicting the target 'label'. No columns have missing values, so no imputation is necessary.

hybrid.name: informal_language_ratio
hybrid.idea: This feature calculates the ratio of informal language markers (e.g., 'u', 'ur', 'r', 'lol', 'omg') in the text. Informal language can indicate casual or personal communication, which might correlate with certain labels, such as spam or personal messages. An n-gram model might capture individual words but not the overall informality level.

hybrid.name: exclamation_density
hybrid.idea: This feature calculates the density of exclamation marks in the text. High exclamation density might indicate excitement, urgency, or spammy content, which could be relevant for classification. An n-gram model might capture the presence of exclamations but not their density relative to text length.

hybrid.name: question_density
hybrid.idea: This feature calculates the density of question marks in the text. High question density might indicate inquiry or conversational tone, which could be relevant for distinguishing between different types of messages. An n-gram model might capture the presence of questions but not their density relative to text length.

hybrid.name: currency_mention_count
hybrid.idea: This feature counts the number of times currency symbols (e.g., $, £, €) are mentioned in the text. Mentions of currency might indicate commercial or spam content, which could be relevant for classification. An n-gram model might capture individual currency symbols but not their overall frequency in a message.

hybrid.name: time_mention_count
hybrid.idea: This feature counts the number of times time expressions (e.g., 'am', 'pm', 'o'clock') are mentioned in the text. Time mentions might indicate scheduling or event-related content, which could be relevant for classification. An n-gram model might capture individual time expressions but not their overall frequency in a message.

proposal.rationale: The target 'label' is a classification target with only 2 unique values ('ham' and 'spam'), indicating a binary classification problem. Log transformation is not applicable to classification targets, which are not continuous and do not have a right-skewed distribution.

plan.overall_rationale: Only the Id column is dropped as it is a pure row identifier with no predictive signal. All other columns are retained, including high-cardinality numeric measurements (e.g., LotArea, GrLivArea) which are valuable continuous features. Missing values in numeric columns are imputed with median/constant defaults reflecting absence of a feature (e.g., 0 for MasVnrArea, GarageYrBlt), while categorical columns with NA-as-category semantics (per housing data dictionary) are filled with explicit 'No<Feature>' labels to preserve information about absence rather than dropping high-missingness columns like PoolQC, Fence, Alley, MiscFeature, and FireplaceQu, since these are informative and AutoGluon can handle categorical NA encoding.

features.reason: Age of house at time of sale is a strong, well-known predictor of price; house age typically correlates negatively with SalePrice.
Years since last remodel captures how 'fresh' the house is, independent of original build year.
Ratio of living area to lot size reflects building density/utilization of the lot, a common derived real-estate metric.
Proportion of basement area relative to above-ground living area may indicate house layout style and value.
Difference highlights how much living space is above vs below ground, potentially informative for pricing.
LotArea is non-negative and typically right-skewed; log transform can stabilize variance and help linear-influenced models.
GrLivArea is non-negative and right-skewed; log transform often improves relationship linearity with log-priced targets.
Basement square footage is non-negative with a skewed distribution (many zeros, long right tail); log1p handles zeros and skew.
Binning overall quality into quantile groups can help capture non-linear threshold effects on price while preserving ordinal signal.
Extracting year from YrSold (already numeric but treated as a date-like field) allows capturing market trend effects over time.
Month of sale can capture seasonal effects on housing prices (e.g., summer vs winter sales).

feature_plan.overall_rationale: The plan focuses on well-established housing-price signals: house age and remodel recency (via differences with YrSold), space utilization ratios (living area vs lot, basement vs living area), and log transforms of skewed, non-negative area features to reduce skew for tree/linear-based ensembling in AutoGluon. Binning OverallQual captures potential non-linear quality thresholds while keeping the original ordinal feature intact. Date parts extraction from YrSold/MoSold captures potential temporal/seasonal market trends. All transformations use existing numeric or date-like columns, avoid ID-like or target columns, and are conservative additions expected to carry genuine signal for SalePrice prediction.

proposal.rationale: SalePrice is a continuous, non-negative regression target with 663 distinct values, min 34900, max 755000. Mean (180921) is noticeably above median (163000), skewness is 1.88 (clearly right-skewed), and max is ~4.6x the median, indicating a long right tail of expensive homes. This is a classic case where log1p transformation converts the objective to relative error, preventing high-priced outliers from dominating squared-error loss, and typically improves regression performance for house price prediction.

plan.overall_rationale: Dropped only the Id column, which is a pure row index with no predictive signal. All other columns, including high-missingness categorical features like PoolQC, Fence, MiscFeature, and Alley, are kept because their missingness is meaningful (absence of a feature) rather than random noise, and AutoGluon can leverage an explicit 'None' category for these. Numeric measurement columns with missing values (LotFrontage, MasVnrArea, GarageYrBlt) are imputed with median (or constant 0 for MasVnrArea, since missing corresponds to no veneer) to preserve their continuous distribution. Categorical basement/garage quality columns are filled with 'None' to reflect the absence of the corresponding structure, consistent with the dataset's known encoding conventions. Electrical's single missing value is filled with the most frequent category. No high-cardinality numeric measurement columns were dropped, per instructions.

features.reason: Age of house at time of sale is a strong predictor of price; captures depreciation/vintage effects better than raw year.
Years since last remodel indicates how 'fresh' the house is, often correlates with sale price.
Ratio of living area to lot size captures building density/utilization, a signal not fully reflected by either variable alone.
Proportion of basement area relative to above-ground living area reflects house layout style, which can influence price.
Difference between above-ground and basement area highlights houses with disproportionate basement vs living space, a structural signal.
LotArea is heavily right-skewed and non-negative; log transform stabilizes variance and helps linear-ish models capture diminishing returns of lot size.
GrLivArea is skewed and strongly tied to price; log transform can linearize its relationship with the (log-scaled) target.
Binning overall quality into quantile groups can help tree models capture non-linear quality tiers while preserving ordinal signal.
Extracting sale year can capture macroeconomic trends (e.g., housing market crash around 2008) affecting price.
Month of sale may reflect seasonal demand patterns influencing sale price.

feature_plan.overall_rationale: The Ames housing dataset benefits from engineered temporal features (house age, remodel recency, sale timing) since raw year columns alone underrepresent depreciation and market timing effects. Ratio and difference features between area-related columns (living area, lot area, basement area) capture spatial layout and density signals that are known strong predictors of house price. Log transforms address right-skew in area-based numeric features to improve model fit, especially for linear-family learners within AutoGluon's ensemble. Binning OverallQual provides an alternative discretized view of an already strong ordinal predictor, potentially helping tree-based learners split more effectively. All proposed features use only non-target, numeric, or date-like columns and avoid ID-like or high-cardinality categorical fields for these numeric transformations.

proposal.rationale: SalePrice is a continuous, non-negative regression target with 663 distinct values. It is clearly right-skewed: mean (180,921) is noticeably above median (163,000), skewness is 1.88, and the max (755,000) is over 4.6x the median with a long tail of expensive homes. This is a classic case where log1p transformation converts squared-error optimization into a relative-error objective, preventing the few expensive houses from dominating the loss, which typically improves regression performance on housing price data.
